ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Yong Tau Foo with Mala Ban combination. FOODON_03315595 (tofu-based dish) is the closest defensible parent anchor. Mala Ban component is not separately represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (tofu dish) is the closest defensible parent code. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Yong Tau Foo or Mala Ban combination dishes. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Yong Tau Foo or Mala Ban combination dish. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: Mala sauce (chili oil, Sichuan peppercorn, bean paste, garlic, ginger, soy sauce)

serving.description: 1 bowl (8 pieces YTF + Mala Ban with noodles or rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising 8 pieces of Yong Tau Foo (stuffed tofu and vegetables) tossed in Mala Ban sauce, served with a choice of noodles or rice.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Yong Tau Foo and composite dish components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for the exact combination of 8-piece Yong Tau Foo with Mala Ban. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for Yong Tau Foo (boiled and fried pieces) and Mala-style sauce components. Input per_100g values (energy 136.36 kcal, protein 6.36 g, fat 8.18 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g) are consistent with HPB Foodo composite estimates for this dish type and are retained with medium confidence. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tofu, fish paste, and chili oil were used to cross-validate secondary micronutrient values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — mixed noodle and tofu dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo with Mala Ban. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries: rice vermicelli / bee hoon (GI ~58), soft tofu (GI ~15), and mixed vegetable dishes (GI ~40–55). A weighted composite GI of approximately 52 is defensible given the dominant noodle/rice base and high protein/fat content from tofu and fish paste, which attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (55 g at 550 g portion × 10 g/100g) / 100 = 52 × 55 / 100 = 28.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100 g; a 550 g serving delivers approximately 2,200 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced Mala sauce, bean paste, and soy sauce to lower per-serving sodium below 1,500 mg, and avoid adding condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 10 g/100 g with estimated GI of 52 (low); protein 6.36 g/100 g from tofu and fish paste supports glycaemic stability, but a 550 g serving delivers approximately 55 g carbohydrate and a glycaemic load of 28.6.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should choose bee hoon or konjac noodles over yellow noodles to reduce the glycaemic load, limit the noodle or rice portion, and select bitter gourd as a YTF piece to further support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.73 g/100 g and trans fat 0.09 g/100 g from deep-fried tau pok and chili oil; sodium 400 mg/100 g elevates cardiovascular risk at full-serving quantities.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit deep-fried YTF pieces (tau pok) to one or two, request reduced Mala sauce to lower saturated fat and sodium intake, and choose a steamed or boiled noodle base over fried options.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried pieces generate advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; sodium 400 mg/100 g is at the RA threshold; ginger and garlic in Mala sauce are evidence-based anti-inflammatory agents.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit deep-fried tau pok to one or two pieces to reduce AGE exposure, request reduced Mala sauce to lower sodium, and substitute nightshade ingredients (chili, brinjal) if nightshade sensitivity is present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo with Mala Ban (8 pieces) is a popular Singaporean combination dish that pairs eight pieces of Yong Tau Foo — stuffed tofu and vegetables filled with fish paste — with a Sichuan-inspired Mala Ban dry-toss sauce. The Yong Tau Foo pieces are typically boiled or deep-fried and may include tau pok (fried tofu puffs), bitter gourd, brinjal, chili, and mushrooms. The Mala Ban component involves blanching the selected ingredients and tossing them in a spicy and numbing sauce made from chili oil, Sichuan peppercorn (málà), fermented bean paste, garlic, ginger, and soy sauce. The dish is served with a choice of noodles (bee hoon, yellow noodles, or konjac noodles) or steamed rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 136 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 8.2 g fat (2.7 g saturated), and 10 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 400 mg/100 g — a clinically significant level for hypertension management. The estimated glycaemic index is 52 (low), making it moderately suitable for Type 2 Diabetes when noodle type and portion are controlled. The dish is rich in plant protein and soy isoflavones from tofu, and the Mala sauce contributes bioactive compounds including gingerols and allicin with anti-inflammatory properties. It is not suitable for strict halal diets without verified halal-certified ingredients. The combination of deep-fried components and high-sodium sauce makes it a moderate cardiovascular concern at full serving sizes.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for the exact combination of 8-piece Yong Tau Foo with Mala Ban. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo composite estimates for Yong Tau Foo and Mala-style sauce components, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tofu, fish paste, and chili oil. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature of the dish and the absence of a direct laboratory measurement.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Yong Tau Foo with Mala Ban dish are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish are not reported in HPB Foodo; estimated ~210 mg/100 g from component analysis but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered as a numeric value. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this composite dish in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 4. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed under HPB Singapore; this is accepted as the HPB Foodo portal. 5. The input listed two portions both labelled '1 bowl' with different weights (650 g and 550 g); 550 g is adopted as the default serving size as the more conservative and commonly cited hawker portion for 8-piece YTF. 6. The input cultural tag 'Laksa' has been removed as it is not relevant to this dish. Primary cuisine corrected from 'Malaysian' to 'Singaporean-Chinese / Sichuan-influenced' reflecting the dish's Singapore hawker origin. 7. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.09 g/100 g from the input source; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with deep-fried components and is retained. 8. Halal status is marked false as Mala sauce ingredients (bean paste, chili oil) require halal certification verification that is not confirmed by the input source. 9. foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type corrected from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. 10. Aliases self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. All 18 flat alias entries account for all aliases_by_language values.
